critic_round1_raw: Second examination. The design specifies cross-entropy loss weighted by inverse class frequency for the transformer. For 10-15 resolution categories with rare emerging types, does inverse frequency weighting on the full training set (months 1-18) correctly represent the frequency distribution in the test set (months 25-36)? If new categories emerge in the test window, their training-set frequencies would be near zero, leading to very high (potentially destabilizing) weights.
defender_round1_raw: The inverse frequency weighting is computed from the training set, which is the correct procedure. Newly emerging categories that appear in the test set but not the training set would not be in the label space at all (since the model cannot predict categories it hasn't seen). Categories that are rare in training but present are correctly upweighted. For genuinely new categories (first appearing in months 25-36 only), they cannot be evaluated as the model has no representation of them. The macro-F1 metric would reflect this - per-class F1 for unseen categories would be zero, and this would be visible in the per-class analysis. This is a scope limitation, not a methodological flaw.
critic_round2_raw: Accepting: newly emerging categories that appear only in the test set are an inherent limitation of supervised classification, not a flaw in this design. The temporal analysis of macro-F1 across 3-month bins within the test window would reveal degradation if new categories emerge. The design acknowledges this in its goals (rare and emerging categories). No remaining flaw. [Forced: no additional substantive methodological concern identified.]
defender_round2_raw: Correct. The design is robust to this concern given its evaluation structure.
adjudication_raw: Forced multiround run 2: Design confirmed sound. Verdict: defense_wins.
